author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
There's a load of reasons skateboards are made the shape they are "conventionally".  Loads of designs have been tried, including stuff that's *way* further off the beaten path than yours.  To my eye, the centre of gravity is still way too high, and the curves, whilst providing a place that ore or less positively locks your feet in, effectively disallow any other placement, which is - well - daft.  If you'd ever ridden a skateboard for any length of time, you'd understand why - skateboard is not a static discipline.  there are cases were static foot placement is forced, kiteboarding and offroad boards, for example, but that's mainly because of the need to be strapped in.

If you want something interesting as a base, try the dirtsurfer.  Should be right up your street - uses old bike parts, and I personally think the frame design could benefit from the torsion spring effect of laminated construction rather than just using steel tube.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
I'll take you at your word re: prototyping suggestions (I appreciate how you've treated mine in past).  So how about that bulletproof quality?  You guys at fwg were doing some research into lightweight, flameproof armour using casein/graphene/ceramics several years ago, why not combine something like that with a strong fabric or cellulose fiber for fabricating variform, contoured composite panels like this example?  graphene plastic with carbon/kevlar might be a fantastic material, and using cellulose fiber (recycled, ideally) even more attractive.  
Another good project Robert, thanks!

author: @MisterRorschach90
text:
Kevlar infused carbon fiber isn’t actually “bulletproof” sorry to be that guy. However it’s definitely strong enough to stop a bullet if you laid up enough layers with the right resin. The carbon fiber base is just too brittle and not hard enough to break the rounds up. 
Sorry I hate being that guy. Bullet resistant materials is what got me into material science and mechanical engineering. I really enjoyed majoring in chemical engineering, but boy I would have had even more fun if I knew that’s what I enjoyed more. There’s just something about layering different materials for composites, testing different resins, patterns, etc. it gets me going. I can work on it for 48 hours straight without stopping and not even realize it. I wish I could somehow get into that industry.

replies.author: @MisterRorschach90
replies.text: Technically a composite without a hard metallic or ceramic surface stops the round by delaminating. The Kevlar is strong enough and resistant enough to hold together and catch the round even when bonded with a resin. Carbon fiber just breaks and splinters. So it delaminates but it also breaks through. This is why carbon fiber isn’t normally used in bullet proof armor. It’s more of a decorative thing. Though I wouldn’t be surprised if someone somewhere is using it in some way or another in combination with something.

replies.author: @Peter_Schluss-Mit-Lustig
replies.text:
@MisterRorschach90 isn't the important factor in this system the Impact-resistance of the material?
Or are there other equally important factors

replies.author: @MisterRorschach90
replies.text: @Peter_Schluss-Mit-Lustig abrasion resistance. Load baring. There’s a couple more off the top of my head.
